industries.name: High-Coercivity NdFeB Magnets
uses: Dysprosium is added to NdFeB permanent magnets (typically 1–6% by weight) to increase coercivity (resistance to demagnetization) at elevated temperatures — the Dy replaces some Nd in the Nd₂Fe₁₄B crystal structure at grain boundaries, increasing the anisotropy field

EV traction motors and hybrid vehicle drive motors operate at 150–180°C and require high-coercivity NdFeB magnets with dysprosium content to prevent demagnetization during normal operation — each EV motor typically requires 0.5–1.5 kg of dysprosium
Wind turbine direct-drive generators using NdFeB magnets in offshore installations require dysprosium additions for temperature stability and long-term reliability in inaccessible locations
The automotive electrification megatrend is the primary driver of dysprosium demand growth — millions of EVs produced annually each containing Dy-enhanced magnets

Grain boundary diffusion (GBD) technology reduces Dy consumption by 50–70% versus bulk substitution — a critical development for supply chain security as dysprosium production is 99%+ from Chinese ionic clay rare earth deposits

companies.name: Shin-Etsu Chemical
companies.use: World's largest NdFeB magnet producer and major dysprosium consumer. Pioneered grain boundary diffusion technology to reduce Dy use per magnet while maintaining coercivity for EV motor and industrial applications.

companies.name: TDK Corporation
companies.use: Major NdFeB magnet manufacturer using dysprosium in high-coercivity grades for automotive, industrial servo, and wind turbine applications. Operates rare earth magnet plants in Japan and China.

companies.name: Toyota Motor Corporation
companies.use: Major EV and HEV manufacturer and dysprosium consumer. Has invested in dysprosium supply security by funding rare earth mining projects in Vietnam and other non-Chinese sources. Each Toyota HEV traction motor contains dysprosium-enhanced NdFeB magnets.

companies.name: China Northern Rare Earth (CNRE)
companies.use: China's largest rare earth producer. Controls significant heavy rare earth (dysprosium, terbium) production from ionic clay deposits in Jiangxi, Fujian, and Guangdong provinces. China produces ~99% of global dysprosium supply.

industries.name: Nuclear Reactor Control
uses: Dysprosium titanate (Dy₂TiO₅) and dysprosium oxide (Dy₂O₃) as burnable neutron absorbers in nuclear fuel — dysprosium has a high neutron absorption cross-section and can be incorporated into control rod materials and fuel pellets
Dysprosium in control rod alloys: Dy-Hf alloys for experimental reactor control applications where conventional B₄C rods are insufficient
Dy₂O₃ as a burnable absorber in high-burnup nuclear fuel designs — absorbs neutrons at beginning of cycle, burns up before cycle end, allowing more fuel flexibility

companies.name: Westinghouse Electric
companies.use: Major nuclear fuel assembly manufacturer. Uses dysprosium-bearing burnable absorbers in advanced fuel designs for high-burnup operation in PWR and BWR reactors at US and international nuclear plants.

industries.name: Specialty Alloys & Research
uses: Dysprosium in Terfenol-D magnetostrictive material (Tb₀.₃Dy₀.₇Fe₂): dysprosium component optimizes the magnetostriction of Terfenol-D for room-temperature operation — Dy lowers the spin-reorientation temperature to below room temperature, maximizing the magnetostrictive effect at ambient conditions

Dysprosium iodide in high-intensity metal halide lamps: produces a warm white light spectrum matching the infrared output of the sun — used in film and studio lighting for color-accurate scene illumination
Dysprosium compounds in data storage: Dy is used in magneto-optical recording media alloys (DyFe, DyFeCo) for high-density archival storage applications
Dysprosium as dopant in calcium fluoride (CaF₂:Dy) thermoluminescent dosimeters for personal radiation monitoring — Dy doping produces an efficient TLD response for gamma and X-ray dose measurement

companies.name: GE Lighting (Savant / Current)
companies.use: Historically a major producer of dysprosium iodide-containing metal halide lamps for studio, architectural, and commercial lighting. Transition to LED has reduced but not eliminated demand for Dy-containing specialty lamps.
